perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other writ ten, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Sensitive Information within a reasonable time period if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Sensitive Information within 90 days of Disclosing Party's verbal request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe to '90 days of verbal request' could contradict laws requiring prompt return of company property upon termination or request, especially if 'reasonable time period' is legally interpreted to be shorter or if a written record of the request is required.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FLSA primarily deals with minimum wage and overtime, the 'reasonable time period' is often interpreted in conjunction with state laws on final paychecks and return of company property, and setting it at 90 days, especially with a verbal request, could unreasonably delay the return and indirectly impact an employee's ability to seek new employment or access necessary documentation. This can be viewed as an unfair labor practice. The obligation for a written request and a 90-day timeframe introduces conditions that delay the return of materials. Many states have specific deadlines about when employers must provide the final paycheck, and holding the employee's materials can interfere with that deadline. The employee has the right to receive payment and documentation without undue delay.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 8 - FAIR LABOR STANDARDS Sec. 206 - Minimum wage From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §206. Minimum wage (a) Employees engaged in commerce; home workers in Puerto Rico and Virgin Islands; employees in American Samoa; seamen on American vessels; agricultural employees Every employer shall pay to each of his employees who in any workweek is engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, or is employed in an enterprise engaged in commerce or in the production of goods for commerce, wages at the following rates: (1) except as otherwise provided in this section, not less than— (A) $5.85 an hour, beginning on the 60th day after May 25, 2007; (B) $6.55 an hour, beginning 12 months after that 60th day; and (C) $7.25 an hour, beginning 24 months after that 60th day; (2) if such employee is a home worker in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands, not less than the minimum piece rate prescribed by regulation or order; or, if no such minimum piece rate is in effect, any piece rate adopted by such employer which shall yield, to the proportion or class of employees prescribed by regulation or order, not less than the applicable minimum hourly wage rate. Such minimum piece rates or employer piece rates shall be commensurate with, and shall be paid in lieu of, the minimum hourly wage rate applicable under the provisions of this section. The Administrator, or his authorized representative, shall have power to make such regulations or orders as are necessary or appropriate to carry out any of the provisions of this paragraph, including the power without limiting the generality of the foregoing, to define any operation or occupation which is performed by such home work employees in Puerto Rico or the Virgin Islands; to establish minimum piece rates for any operation or occupation so defined; to prescribe the method and procedure for ascertaining and promulgating minimum piece rates; to prescribe standards for employer piece rates, including the proportion or class of employees who shall
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The federal minimum wage is $7.25 per hour effective July 24, 2009. Many states also have minimum wage laws. Some state laws provide greater employee protections; employers must comply with both. The FLSA does not provide wage payment collection procedures for an employee’s usual or promised wages or commissions in excess of those required by the FLSA. However, some states do have laws under which such claims (sometimes including fringe benefits) may be filed. perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction by altering the 'reasonable time period' to a fixed '90 days of verbal request'. This directly contradicts the concept of a 'reasonable time period' because the LLM's explanation suggests that the verbal request and the 90-day timeframe introduce conditions that could delay the return of materials unreasonably and could interfere with deadlines. Although the scraped snippets primarily address minimum wage aspects of the FLSA, the Law Explanation refers to states having deadlines for providing final paychecks and documentation. By potentially delaying the return of necessary materials, the 90-day period could interfere with those deadlines, creating a legal issue. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction,' where a time-sensitive obligation (returning materials) does not align with legal requirements for promptness, as inferred from the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The non -disclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Sensitive Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Sensitive Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or otherwise needing protection until Disclosing Party send s Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whicheve r occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: The non-disclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Sensitive Information in confidence shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: An indefinite non-disclosure period may be deemed unenforceable in some jurisdictions as an unreasonable restraint of trade, conflicting with laws that protect an individual's ability to use their knowledge and skills.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. An indefinite non-disclosure agreement could be interpreted as such a restraint, particularly if it prevents the Receiving Party from using general knowledge or skills acquired during their relationship with the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a specific condition for termination of the non-disclosure agreement, making it indefinite. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, as explained by the LLM, because an indefinite NDA could restrain someone from engaging in their lawful profession, trade, or business. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing BPC 16600, states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The original text had conditions for the NDA's termination, while the changed text removes these conditions, creating a contradiction with the law's intent to prevent unreasonable restraints of trade.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to Sensitive Information to employees, contractors and third parties as is reasonably required and shall require those persons to sign nondisclosure restrictions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall restrict access to Sensitive Information solely to its executive officers and require no further nondisclosure agreements from them.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting access only to executive officers without requiring NDAs could violate data protection laws if those officers are not adequately trained in data security or if the company fails to implement reasonable security measures, as it assumes implicit confidentiality without explicit documented agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 32 requires controllers and processors to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including ensuring that persons authorised to process personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality. By restricting access to only executive officers without any explicit NDAs, the Receiving Party may fail to demonstrate that appropriate measures are in place to protect the confidentiality of Sensitive Information, potentially violating GDPR if the information contains personal data of EU citizens.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original clause by restricting access to Sensitive Information solely to executive officers without requiring any NDAs. The original text mandated NDAs at least as protective as those in the agreement for all employees, contractors, and third parties with access. The Law Explanation points to GDPR Article 32, which requires appropriate measures to ensure confidentiality for those processing personal data. Although the scraped snippets do not directly quote Article 32, the Law Explanation's reference to it suggests that limiting access without NDAs fails to demonstrate appropriate measures for data protection. This creates a legal contradiction because the modified text removes a critical safeguard (NDAs) that helps ensure confidentiality, potentially violating data protection laws like GDPR if the information involves EU citizens' personal data. The Perturbation Explanation further supports this, noting that the absence of NDAs could violate data protection laws if executive officers aren't adequately trained in data security.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement expresses the complete understanding of the partie s with respect to the subject matter and supersedes all prior proposals, agreements, representations and understandings.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement expresses the complete understanding of the parties with respect to the subject matter, but any prior oral agreements shall take precedence over the terms outlined herein.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that prior oral agreements take precedence directly contradicts the purpose of an integration clause, which is to ensure that the written agreement is the final and complete agreement. Prior oral agreements are not written and can be impossible to prove, making the entire contract unenforceable.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state (e.g., New York General Obligations Law § 5-701)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts, such as those involving the sale of land or agreements that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing to be enforceable. By stating that prior oral agreements take precedence, the modified text creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability, especially if those oral agreements relate to matters that fall under the Statute of Frauds. This undermines the purpose of having a written agreement at all and contradicts the principles of contract law related to the statute of frauds.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intent of the agreement by stating that prior oral agreements take precedence over the written terms. This undermines the purpose of having a written agreement, especially considering the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable as highlighted in both snippets. Snippet 1 explicitly states, 'Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing...' while Snippet 2 clarifies that the statute of frauds 'is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury.' Allowing oral agreements to supersede the written contract creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability, as these oral agreements may not meet the requirements of the Statute of Frauds.
